claim: For all integers n > 1, if n is prime then 2^n - 2 is divisible by n.

claim: For all odd primes n, 2^(n-1) - 1 is divisible by n.
For all integers n > 1, if n is prime then its Euler totient phi(n) = n-1 satisfies 2^phi(n) == 1 mod n for gcd(2,n)=1.

claim: Euler's Theorem: For all integers n > 1, if gcd(a, n) = 1, then a^phi(n) is 1 mod n.
predicate: (abs(n) <= 1) or all(pow(a, phi(abs(n)), abs(n)) == 1 for a in [3, 5] if gcd(a, abs(n)) == 1)

claim: If n is prime, then n divides (n-1)! + 1 (Wilson's Theorem).
For prime n > 2, (n-1)! is an even integer.
The expression (1+x)^n - (1+x^n) is divisible by n for all prime n.

claim: If n is a prime of the form 4k+1, then 2 is a quadratic residue mod n if n is also +/- 1 mod 8.
For n > 1, n is prime if and only if gcd(n, (n-1)!) = 1.
For any prime n, the sum of divisors sigma(n) is n+1.
For any prime n, sigma(n) + phi(n) = 2n.
If n is prime, then n divides sigma(n) + phi(n).
For prime p >= 5, p^2 divides the numerator of the (p-1)-th harmonic sum (Wolstenholme's Theorem).

claim: If n is prime, then the number of ways to choose k objects from n is always a multiple of n for 1 <= k <= n-1.

For n > 3, if n is prime, then n divides (3^(n-1) - 1).
For prime n > 3, n divides 6^(n-1) - 1.
If n is a perfect square, sigma(n) is odd.
If n is prime and n > 2, then sigma(n) is even.
For any prime n, the product phi(n) * sigma(n) = n^2 - 1.
If phi(n) * sigma(n) = n^2 - 1, then n is prime.
For prime n > 2, phi(n) * sigma(n) is a multiple of 4.

For any prime p, the number of divisors of p^k is k+1.

claim: For prime p > 3, either p-1 or p+1 is divisible by 6.
For prime p > 3, the product (p-1)(p+1) is always divisible by 24.
For prime p > 3, phi(p) * sigma(p) is a multiple of 24.

The central binomial coefficient C(2n, n) is divisible by 2 raised to the sum of bits of n.
C(2n, n) is even for all n >= 1.
For prime p, p divides C(2p, p) - 2.
For prime p >= 5, C(2p, p) is congruent to 2 modulo p^3.
